title: Throwing a desk pencil holder
subtitle: A simple thrown cylinder in stoneware: a good first cylinder project
excerpt: A thrown stoneware cylinder 120 mm tall and 80 mm in diameter, glazed inside and out, fired to cone 6 (1220 degrees C). Straightforward walls, no lid, no trimmed foot-ring required. A practical first cylinder project for wheel beginners.

glossaryTerms.definition: The stage at which clay holds its shape but remains damp enough to trim and join
Fully air-dried unfired clay; fragile before bisque firing
The first kiln firing (cone 06, approx 1000 degrees C) that hardens greenware into porous bisqueware
Pyrometric measure of heat-work in a kiln. Cone 06 approx 1000 degrees C, cone 6 approx 1220 degrees C, cone 10 approx 1280 degrees C
The trimmed-back base ridge a finished pot sits on

content.text: Silica dust is the chronic studio hazard. Silicosis is irreversible. Mix wet whenever possible. Wear a P100 respirator any time raw glaze powders are open. Wet-clean only: no sweeping, no dry sanding indoors.

Ensure adequate ventilation when firing: a downdraft vent or open window with an extractor. Wear welder's shade-5 glasses when peephole-watching at high temperatures. Do not open a hot kiln below 80 degrees C. Keep a Class A fire extinguisher within reach.

A cylinder is the foundation of almost every thrown form. This pencil holder is a practical first project that builds the core skills: centering, opening, and pulling straight walls without flaring or narrowing. At 120 mm tall and 80 mm in diameter, it is achievable with 400 g of smooth stoneware in a single session. It glazes inside and out and fires to
cone
6 (1220 degrees C). There is no lid to fit, no trimmed
foot-ring
required if you prefer a flat base: just clean the base edge and go. Focus on the walls.

materials.name: Mid-fire glaze, pre-mixed
materials.quantity: enough for two to three brush coats inside and out

content.text: Preparation

Wedge 400 g of smooth stoneware to remove air pockets and achieve a uniform texture. Wedging prevents explosion during firing from trapped air. Wet the wheel head lightly and centre the clay directly on the wheel rather than on a bat: a small cylinder this size is easy to wire off cleanly.
Centring

Spin the wheel at medium-high speed and push the clay inward from both sides with the heels of both hands simultaneously. The clay wobble should reduce to nothing. Apply light downward pressure as well as inward. Take your time at this stage: a poorly centred piece cannot be pulled into a straight cylinder.

Cone the clay up by pressing inward with both hands around the mass and squeezing upward, then push back down with the heel of one hand. Repeat twice. This further aligns the clay particles and confirms the centre is true.
Opening and pulling

Open from the centre by pressing two or three fingers straight down to within 8 mm of the wheel head, then drag outward to create the floor. The opening diameter should be approximately 50 mm, leaving clay for the walls on either side.
Compress the floor with the flat of the wooden rib immediately after opening.

Pull the walls up in two or three passes: position the outside hand fingers at the base of the outside wall and the inside hand fingers directly opposite on the inside. Squeeze gently and move both hands upward together at the same rate. Each pull should move the hands from the bottom to the rim in one smooth motion. Aim for walls 7 to 8 mm thick.

Keep the walls vertical: a pencil holder that flares outward loses its capacity for long pencils. Check the profile by eye from the side between each pulling pass and use the outside fingers to push the wall back inward if it has bowed out.

Smooth the rim with the chamois leather. Clean the inside base with a small sponge. Check the height with the needle tool held vertically alongside the form: aim for 120 mm. If the form is slightly shorter, do one more gentle pulling pass.
Wire the cylinder off the wheel head with the wire cutter held flat against the wheel surface. Slide it onto a board and leave to dry to
leather-hard
before the optional trimming step.
Optional trimming

At leather-hard, the base edge can be smoothed and a shallow foot-ring trimmed if preferred. Place the cylinder upright on the banding wheel and use the loop tool to trim a foot-ring approximately 4 mm tall and 6 mm wide. This lifts the base clear of the kiln shelf and gives a cleaner underside. If you prefer a flat-bottomed pencil holder, simply smooth the base edge with a damp sponge and skip trimming.
Bisque and glaze firing
Allow the piece to reach
bone-dry
before
bisque
firing to cone 06 (approximately 1000 degrees C).

Glaze inside and out with the mid-fire glaze. Pour glaze inside and swirl to coat, then pour out. Glaze the outside by dipping or with a mop brush in two to three coats. Wax the base or clean glaze from the base edge before loading the kiln shelf.
Glaze-fire to cone 6 (1220 degrees C). Allow the kiln to cool to below 80 degrees C before opening.
Variations
Taller version: use 600 g and pull the walls to 160 mm for a holder that accommodates rulers and longer brushes.
Pulled handle: attach a small pulled handle at leather-hard to convert the holder into a small mug-style desk caddy.
Carved rim: at leather-hard, use the fettling knife to cut a notched or scalloped pattern around the top edge before bisque firing.

rows.cause: The outside hand was not applying enough inward pressure to counteract the centrifugal force
rows.fix: On the next pull, actively press the outside wall inward as the hands move upward. Keep both hands at the same height throughout the pull.

rows.cause: Too much water used during throwing, weakening the top of the walls; or the final pull went too high
rows.fix: Use minimal water: re-wet only when the clay starts to drag. Slow the wheel for the final pass and support the inside rim with a fingertip as you compress the top.

rows.problem: Base too thin after wiring off
rows.cause: Opening went too deep, leaving insufficient floor thickness
rows.fix: Check floor thickness with the needle tool before pulling. The floor should feel solid and even when tapped with a finger.
